input_excerpt:
James Carter

Jacksonville, NC · (910) 555-0148 · jcarter88@email.com

SUMMARY

U.S. Marine Corps veteran with six years of experience leading teams, managing equipment, and getting results under pressure. Known for reliability, discipline, and strong leadership. Looking to bring these skills to a civilian team in operations, logistics, or a related field.

EXPERIENCE
Team Leader / Sergeant (E-5),

findings.title: Clearance currency unverifiable
findings.why: The resume shows separation in 2025. Clearances lapse without a sponsoring position, and a recruiter who checks JPAS/DISS will read an inaccurate 'Active' as either carelessness or inflation — on the one line where precision matters most for operations and logistics employers.
findings.task: State only the status you can verify today, with its date (for example, eligibility within the reinstatement window). Confirm the true current status before listing it.

findings.title: Asserted traits carry no evidence
findings.quote: Known for reliability, discipline, and strong leadership.
findings.why: 'Known for' asks the reader to take your word for it. A civilian hiring reader treats unverifiable trait claims as filler and skips them — this spends prime summary space proving nothing.
findings.task: Cut this sentence or replace it with one specific outcome your leadership produced that a bullet on the page can back up. You decide which fact best fits the target role.

findings.title: Vague scope on core duty
findings.quote: Supervised and trained a team of up to 12 people, managing their performance, safety, and daily tasks.
findings.why: 'Up to 12' and 'managing daily tasks' leave a civilian operations reader unable to gauge your actual authority or the consistency of your span of control. 'Up to' signals a peak, not a norm, and reads as inflation.
findings.task: State the team size you consistently led and the one supervisory responsibility most relevant to the target role. Draft the revision yourself using numbers you can defend.

findings.title: Equipment claim lacks operating context
findings.quote: Maintained 100% accountability of equipment valued at over $500,000 with no losses.
findings.why: 'Accountability' in a civilian logistics context could mean inventory control, signed custody, or physical security — the reader cannot tell which, or what your decision authority was. The dollar figure alone does not show the process you ran.
findings.task: Name what you actually did to maintain accountability (tracking method, custody role, audit outcome) so a logistics reader can map it to inventory or asset-management work. Verify the figure is one you can document.

findings.title: Outcome-free planning claim
findings.quote: Planned and carried out operations and training, consistently meeting deadlines and standards.
findings.why: 'Operations and training' is undefined to a civilian, and 'meeting deadlines and standards' is the baseline expectation, not an achievement. The reader learns nothing about scale, complexity, or result.
findings.task: Pick one planning example and add its observable result (what was produced, for how many, under what constraint). Translate 'operations' into plain terms the target employer uses.

findings.title: Leadership course not translated
findings.why: A civilian reader cannot tell what 'supervisor-level' course covered, how long it ran, or whether it maps to any recognized supervisory credential. As written it reads as an unverifiable placeholder.
findings.task: Name the specific course(s) and the concrete skills covered, or state the duration/format, so the reader can judge relevance. Do not imply civilian certification equivalence unless you can confirm it.

findings.title: Recognition claim is vague
findings.quote: earned recognition for leadership and dependability
findings.why: Unnamed 'recognition' reads as filler, especially when your Awards section already lists specific medals. A civilian reader discounts unattributed praise.
findings.task: Either tie this to the specific award it refers to or cut it to avoid duplicating the Awards section. You decide which is stronger.

note: The draft leans on unverifiable traits and an 'active' clearance claim while leaving scope, authority, and outcomes too vague for a civilian operations or logistics reader to assess; tighten currency, evidence, and translation first.
